seo.title: Ludo Cards | Neelanzone
seo.description: A game design case study exploring how Ludo can feel more strategic, intentional, and replayable through stronger player agency.

sections.title: Ludo Cards
summary: Card based redesign of classic ludo, immersed in lore, rewarding player agency and strategic gameplay.

media.alt: Animated gameplay preview for Ludo Cards.

hero.title: Ludo Cards

tabs.label: Problem
tabs.body: Classic ludo leaves too much to chance. Your ability to exit the base, move, attack, defend - all comes down to a single dice roll. The only real choice a player makes is which pawn to move. In an age of Catan, Monopoly Deal, D&D - a tabletop legend has taken a backseat.

tabs.label: Approach
tabs.body: The goal was to introduce meaningful choices without dismantling the game. RPG mechanics, narrative framing, and deck-based abilities rewards foresight and planning instead of a single lucky throw. Lore was added to make players care about the lives of pawns they otherwise so casually sacrificed at the altar of chance.

tabs.label: Outcome
tabs.body: Players think beyond their dice rolls and reconsider moves based on what cards others might be holding. Metagame manipulators shift the social texture of the game. Suddenly you're not just playing the board, you're playing the table.

tabs.label: Constraint
tabs.body: The question that kept the project grounded - If you have to explain more than two minutes, is it still ludo? Engineering the feeling that your choices mattered, even when the dice disagrees, while keeping it casual - is the challenge.

hero.paragraph: Card based redesign of classic ludo, immersed in lore, rewarding player agency and strategic gameplay.

quotes.text: I do not feel like I have control over the board, you roll a dice and choose which token to move. That is all.
It's frustrating to wait for a 1 or 6 to get my pawn out, feels like I'm losing before the game even begins.
When someone takes out my token right before the safe passage to the finish, I'm helpless.
I'd love a reason to care about which pawn to move, not just which one is safest.

rightVisual.alt: Fan of action group cards from the Ludo RPG concept.

featureMedia.alt: Temporary gameplay screen placeholder for the Ludo Cards prototype.
featureMedia.caption: screengrab of a gameplay in progress - under development

items.title: Narrative Design
items.body: The building blocks of a story were already hiding in Classic Ludo: a journey, factions, contested ground, and moments of reversal. Naming the board as a world made movement feel less abstract and more like traversal.

expandedColumns.title: Worldbuilding Hooks
expandedColumns.body: Terrain names turned each lane into a setting cue, which helped movement feel more like traversal through regions than a color-coded sprint.

expandedColumns.title: Narrative Payoff
expandedColumns.body: That framing opened room for quests, regional hazards, and card abilities that could feel anchored to the world instead of randomly assigned to a pawn.

items.title: Board Game Design
items.body: Ludo Cards adds planning, role identity, and risk-reward decisions to a loop that is usually resolved by pure probability. The aim was not to replace Ludo, but to reveal a more tactical version inside it.

expandedColumns.title: Decision Density
expandedColumns.body: Cards and character classes create reasons to commit to one move over another, so a turn feels authored rather than merely resolved.

expandedColumns.title: Readable Tradeoffs
expandedColumns.body: Abilities had to stay legible at a glance. The system works best when aggression, defense, and mobility each create a visible cost.

items.title: Digital Development
items.body: Playtesting the physical version revealed a bookkeeping problem. Once characters, action cards, traps, and board events layered together, players needed a digital surface to hold the complexity cleanly.

expandedColumns.title: Why Go Digital
expandedColumns.body: The digital surface reduces rules friction, keeps board state visible, and makes richer systems easier to test with real players.

expandedColumns.title: What Comes Next
expandedColumns.body: The web prototype becomes the proving ground for class balance, pacing, and how multiplayer coordination should feel when the system scales.

items.title: Expansion Pack
items.body: A printable add-on deck that lets the concept move off the screen and back onto the table.

items.title: Web App Prototype
items.body: A multiplayer-friendly prototype where card logic, character states, and board events can all live in one readable system.

items.title: Reflections
items.body: Notes on constraints, tradeoffs, and the moments where the system got more complicated than the board could comfortably hold.
